ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general sausage food product class; no specific cheese sausage subclass exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers sausages and similar products; no cheese-specific sausage subcode exists in the published hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for cheese sausage as a food product; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single cheese sausage link weighs approximately 60 g and is commonly consumed as a breakfast item, snack, or side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Cheese Sausage — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values preserved as reported. Saturated fat not directly reported by HPB for this entry; estimated at 9.5 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central NDB 07068 (pork and cheese sausage) as a secondary cross-reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cheese sausage in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 28 (low) based on analogous processed meat sausage entries in the Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Processed meat sausages with low carbohydrate content (≤6 g/100g) consistently yield low GI values. Glycemic load per 60 g serving = (28 × 6.0 × 0.60) / 100 = 1.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 806 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold; cured with sodium nitrite
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid cheese sausage or strictly limit intake to ≤30 g as an occasional flavour accent, as a single 100 g serving delivers 806 mg sodium — more than one-third of the 2000 mg daily limit — and sodium nitrites in cured meats independently elevate cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Ultra-processed meat with high saturated fat (~9.5 g/100g) worsening insulin resistance; regular processed meat consumption associated with increased T2DM risk
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although carbohydrates are low (6 g/100g) and the glycemic index is low, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit cheese sausage to occasional small portions (≤50 g) because the high saturated fat content worsens insulin resistance over time and regular processed meat consumption is independently associated with increased T2DM incidence per ADA guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~9.5 g/100g), trans fat (0.1 g/100g), sodium (806 mg/100g), and sodium nitrites — all established cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of cheese sausage, as its combination of high saturated fat, trans fat, very high sodium, and sodium nitrites collectively raises LDL cholesterol, promotes endothelial dysfunction, and elevates blood pressure; if consumed, limit to ≤30 g infrequently and balance with high-fibre, low-sodium foods.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium nitrites, high saturated fat, and trans fat are pro-inflammatory and exacerbate RA-related joint inflammation and tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid cheese sausage, as sodium nitrites, saturated fat (~9.5 g/100g), and trans fat (0.1 g/100g) promote TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine activity, while high sodium (806 mg/100g) worsens RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cheese sausage is an ultra-processed meat product consisting of an emulsified and cured meat matrix — typically pork or chicken — with cheese either blended into the meat mixture or used as a filling. It is manufactured by combining ground meat, fat, starch, salt, sodium nitrite, spices, and cheese, then stuffing the mixture into a casing and cooking or smoking it. The texture is firm yet yielding, with a savoury, salty, and mildly tangy flavour from the cheese component. Per 100 g, cheese sausage delivers 302 kcal, 15.5 g protein, 24.1 g fat (of which approximately 9.5 g is saturated), 6.0 g carbohydrate, 1.2 g sugar, 1.1 g dietary fibre, 806 mg sodium, 80 mg calcium, and 0.93 mg iron. The very high sodium content (806 mg/100g) makes it strongly unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The high saturated fat and processed meat composition raise concerns for Type 2 Diabetes management and cardiovascular health. It is commonly consumed as a breakfast item, snack, or side dish in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, often grilled, pan-fried, or eaten straight from the packet. The glycemic index is estimated as low (~28) due to minimal carbohydrate content, but this does not offset the metabolic risks associated with regular processed meat consumption.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in cheese sausage?
Is cheese sausage suitable for hypertension?
Is cheese sausage high in fat?
Can I eat cheese sausage if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core per_100g nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous entry (NDB 07068) and documented. GI estimated from analogous processed sausage entries in the University of Sydney GI Database with clear documentation. All values are internally consistent and plausible for an ultra-processed cheese sausage product.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific cheese sausage product are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry, and no sufficiently close match was found in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy to supply these values with confidence. saturated_fat_g (9.5 g/100g) and trans_fat_g (0.1 g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 07068 (pork and cheese sausage) as the closest approved-source analogue; this estimation is documented in nutrient_source.source_match_notes. GI of 28 is an analogous estimate based on processed meat sausage entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); documented in gi_source. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered. Halal status is not confirmed for this generic product category; consumers should verify product-specific halal certification.
